records.ety: [OE. hamer, AS. hamer, hamor; akin to D. hamer, G. & Dan. hammer, Sw. hammare, Icel. hamarr, hammer, crag, and perh. to Gr. 'a`kmwn anvil, Skr. açman stone.]

defs: An instrument for driving nails, beating metals, and the like, consisting of a head, usually of steel or iron, fixed crosswise to a handle.
quotes: With busy hammers closing rivets up.

defs: Something which in form or action resembles the common hammer
That part of a clock which strikes upon the bell to indicate the hour.
The padded mallet of a piano, which strikes the wires, to produce the tones.

That part of a gunlock which strikes the percussion cap, or firing pin; the cock; formerly, however, a piece of steel covering the pan of a flintlock musket and struck by the flint of the cock to ignite the priming.
Also, a person or thing that smites or shatters; as, St. Augustine was the hammer of heresies.
quotes: He met the stern legionaries [of Rome] who had been the “massive iron hammers” of the whole earth.

defs: A spherical weight attached to a flexible handle and hurled from a mark or ring. The weight of head and handle is usually not less than 16 pounds.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Hammered (-mrd); p. pr. & vb. n. Hammering.]

defs: To beat with a hammer; to beat with heavy blows; as, to hammer iron.
To form or forge with a hammer; to shape by beating.
To form in the mind; to shape by hard intellectual labor; -- usually with out.
quotes: Who was hammering out a penny dialogue.

defs: To be busy forming anything; to labor hard as if shaping something with a hammer.
quotes: Whereon this month I have been hammering.

defs: To strike repeated blows, literally or figuratively.
quotes: Blood and revenge are hammering in my head.
